34: prosopolemptes (prosōpolēmptēs, 'one who shows partiality/face-receiver'): a NT coinage from the LXX idiom lambanein prosopon (lift the face = receive favorably), itself a calque of Hebrew nasa' panim. The word appears in Paul (Rom 2:11; Eph 6:9; Col 3:25) and Jas 2:1. Peter's declaration that God is not a prosopolēmptēs marks the theological breakthrough of the Cornelius episode: the ethnic boundary between Jew and Gentile is not the boundary of divine acceptance.
44: epepesen to Pneuma to Hagion (epepesen to Pneuma to Hagion, 'the Holy Spirit fell upon'): the same verb as Acts 2 (2:3, epesen); Luke deliberately signals that Cornelius's household receives the identical Pentecost as the Jerusalem community. epipiptō (fall upon) is striking in its physicality — the Spirit is not merely invited or requested but descends sovereignly, surprising Peter mid-sermon. The circumcised believers are exestēsan (astonished) because the gift precedes any formal completion of conversion rites, vindicating God's impartial acceptance.
1: 'At Caesarea there was a man named Cornelius' — the introduction follows the standard Hellenistic 'person introduction' formula (aner tis... onomati Kornēlios — a certain man named Cornelius). Caesarea Maritima was the Roman administrative capital of Judaea, built by Herod the Great; a centurion stationed there commands approximately eighty men. The 'Italian Regiment' (speira Italikē) is a cohort designation suggesting Roman citizenship and elite status. Luke introduces Cornelius with as much social detail as he gave Lydia (16:14) or Zacchaeus (19:2) — the social register of the first Gentile convert is deliberate.
2: 'Devout and God-fearing' (eusebēs kai phoboumenos ton theon — pious and fearing God) — eusebēs appears only here in Acts; phoboumenos ton theon ('fearing God') is the technical designation for Gentile synagogue-adherents who accepted monotheism and Jewish ethics but had not become full proselytes. 'Gave generously to those in need' (poiōn eleēmosynas — doing acts of mercy/alms) — eleēmosyne is the standard NT word for almsgiving, connected to the Hebrew tsedaqah (righteous/justice-based generosity). 'Prayed to God regularly' (deomenos tou theou dia pantos — entreating God continually) — the combination of alms and prayer is the classic Jewish piety formula.
3: 'He had a vision. He distinctly saw (eiden en horamati phanerōs — he saw in a vision clearly) an angel of God' — the horama (vision) uses the technical term for prophetic/divine visionary experience (Acts 7:31; 9:10, 12; 16:9-10; 18:9). Phanerōs (clearly, openly, distinctly) emphasizes the vision's unambiguous quality — no dreamy vagueness. 'At about three in the afternoon' — the ninth hour (hōran tēs hōras tēs ennatas) is the time of the afternoon sacrifice in the temple — and presumably the time Cornelius was praying (v.30 confirms: 'at three in the afternoon'). The divine vision comes at the prayer-hour.
4: 'Your prayers and gifts to the poor have come up as a memorial offering (anabēsan eis mnēmosunon — have gone up as a reminder) before God' — anabainō (go up) is the standard verb for sacrificial smoke ascending; Cornelius's non-sacrificial offerings are treated as ascending sacrifices. Mnēmosunon (memorial offering, reminder) is the LXX technical term for the 'memorial portion' of grain offerings presented before YHWH (Lev 2:2; 5:12; 6:15; Num 5:26). Luke's language frames Cornelius's Gentile piety in temple-offering categories, legitimizing it before a Jewish audience.
5: 'Simon who is called Peter' — the double name (Simōna ton epikaloumenon Petron) reflects the Aramaic/Greek bilingualism of the early church: Simon (Hebrew/Aramaic) called Peter (Greek translation of the Aramaic Kepha = rock). Luke consistently uses both names, reminding readers that the Greek-speaking church's leadership was rooted in an Aramaic-speaking Palestinian Jewish community. The naming of the town (Joppa) and the host (Simon the tanner, v.6) provides the verifiable detail typical of Luke's historical narration.
6: 'He is staying with Simon the tanner, whose house is by the sea' — the tanner (burseus) was ritually marginal in Jewish society: constant contact with animal hides made Levitical purity-observance difficult; tanners were required to live at the edge of settlements because of the smell. Peter's lodging with a socially marginal artisan already positions him at the boundary of conventional purity-consciousness — a preparation for the rooftop vision that will push him further beyond those boundaries.
7: 'When the angel who spoke to him had gone, Cornelius called two of his servants and a devout soldier (stratiōtēn eusebē — a pious soldier) who was one of his attendants' — the soldier is described with eusebēs (devout/pious), the same word applied to Cornelius in v.2. Cornelius's entire household participates in his God-fearing orientation. The sending of three men (v.8 — three: two servants + one soldier) corresponds to the Spirit's instruction to Peter in v.19 ('three men are looking for you').
8: 'He told them everything and sent them to Joppa' — the panta (everything) that Cornelius tells his servants — the vision, the angel, the instructions — is passed on in full to the sent party. The full communication is significant: these men are not mere messengers carrying a note; they know the divine context of their errand. When they arrive at Peter's house (v.22), their testimony will confirm the divine initiation of the Cornelius-encounter.
9: 'About noon the following day... Peter went up on the roof to pray' — the sixth hour (hōran hektēn) is midday. The rooftop (dōma) was the standard domestic prayer and rest space in Mediterranean houses; it provided elevation, privacy, and air circulation. Peter's midday prayer positions him in a state of regular devotion — the divine vision finds him already in prayer. The Spirit consistently appears to those who are praying in Acts: Cornelius at 3pm prayer (v.3), Peter at noon prayer, the disciples at Pentecost in prayer (1:14).
10: 'He fell into a trance' (epepesen ep' auton ekstasis — an ecstasy fell upon him) — ekstasis is the 'standing-outside-oneself' state; the verb epipiptō (to fall upon) is the same verb used for the Spirit falling upon the disciples (11:15 — 'the Holy Spirit fell on them'). The MKT 'trance' (conventional rendering) captures the dissociative quality but loses the spatial displacement sense of ek-stasis (standing-outside/beside oneself). The 'trance' is not self-induced but falls upon Peter as the Spirit falls upon the church.
11: 'He saw heaven opened and something like a large sheet being let down to earth by its four corners' — the ouranon aneōgmenon (opened heaven) is the standard prophetic-vision frame (Ezek 1:1; John 1:51; Rev 4:1; 19:11). The object (skeuos ti — a certain vessel/object) is described as 'like a large sheet' (othonē — a linen cloth, sail-like material) being lowered by its four corners (tesarsin archais — four beginnings/corners). The fourfold lowering from the four corners of heaven evokes the universalist imagery of gathering from the four winds (Isa 11:12; Ezek 37:9).
12: 'It contained all kinds of four-footed animals, as well as reptiles and birds' (panta ta tetrapoda kai ta herpeta tēs gēs kai ta peteina tou ouranou) — the three-category list (four-footed, reptiles, birds) recapitulates the creation-taxonomy of Gen 1:24-25 but deliberately scrambles the clean/unclean distinctions of Lev 11. The sheet contains 'all kinds' (panta — all) indiscriminately: both clean and unclean together. The vision's content is as significant as its form.
13: 'Get up, Peter. Kill and eat' (Anastas, Petre, thuson kai phage) — the command-form is terse and urgent. Anasta (rise!) is the same verb used in healings and resurrection-announcements (9:34; 9:40). Thuson (kill, slaughter) is the standard sacrificial-slaughter verb, here used for ordinary food preparation. The command to kill-and-eat reverses the Levitical clean-food legislation from within the framework of Jewish vocabulary — not by abolishing the law but by God's direct command overriding specific dietary prohibitions.
14: 'Surely not, Lord!' (Mēdamōs, Kyrie — by no means, Lord!) — mēdamōs is an emphatic negative of refusal; the exclamation appears in LXX in contexts of strong denial (Gen 44:7, 17; Josh 22:29). Peter's 'Lord' (Kyrie) combined with his refusal creates the paradox of disobedient address: he calls Jesus Lord while refusing the Lord's command. 'I have never eaten anything impure or unclean' (koinon ē akatharton — common/defiled or unclean) — koinos (common) has acquired the specialized meaning 'ritually defiled' in Jewish usage; it is the word used for 'common/profane' as opposed to 'set apart/holy.'
15: 'Do not call anything impure that God has made clean' (ha ho theos ekatharisern, su mē koinou — what God cleansed, you do not make common) — the divine passive ekatharisen (has made clean) is decisive: God has performed the cleansing action, not Peter. The counter-command reverses Peter's vocabulary: you used 'common' as a negative; God has used 'clean' as a positive over the same objects. The present-tense command (mē koinou) is continuous: stop making-common what God has cleansed.
16: 'This happened three times, and immediately the sheet was taken back to heaven' — the threefold repetition of the vision (tris — three times) is the standard biblical confirmation number (Gen 40:20; Dan 6:10, 13; Jonah 1:17; Matt 12:40). The triple command and triple refusal mirrors Peter's triple denial (John 18:17, 25, 27) and the triple commissioning (John 21:15-17); three is both the number of divine confirmation and the number of Peter's significant moments. The immediate withdrawal (anelēmphthē — was taken up) ends the vision before Peter has resolved its meaning.
17: 'While Peter was wondering about the meaning of the vision (ti an eiē to horama ho eiden — what the vision he saw might mean)' — the imperfect diēporei (he was greatly perplexed/at a loss) indicates ongoing, unresolved puzzlement. The vision's meaning is not immediately transparent even to the seer. Peter must interpret a symbolic vision while simultaneously acting on its practical direction (the three men are already at the gate). The combination of unresolved theological interpretation and immediate practical obedience is the apostolic pattern.
18: 'They called out, asking if Simon who was known as Peter was staying there' — the arriving messengers' inquiry (epunthanouto — were inquiring, imperfect = continued asking) at the gate coincides precisely with Peter's perplexity. The providential timing (Peter confused about the vision; three men arriving as the vision predicted) is Luke's characteristic demonstration that divine ordering underlies the surface of coincidence. The 'Simon called Peter' double name (v.5, 18) maintains the Aramaic-Greek bilingualism flag.
19: 'While Peter was still thinking about the vision, the Spirit said to him' — the Spirit's direct address (eipen autō to pneuma — the Spirit said to him) identifies the communicating agent as the same Spirit who orchestrated the vision. Pneuma here (disp=3) is capitalized in the MKT as 'Spirit' — the third person of the Trinity communicating directly. 'Three men are looking for you' — the precision confirms that the Spirit knows exactly what is happening at the gate while Peter contemplates the rooftop vision.
20: 'So get up and go downstairs. Do not hesitate to go with them, for I have sent them' (mē diakrinomenos — not discriminating/doubting) — diakrinō carries both the sense of 'doubt' and 'discriminate/make distinctions.' The MKT 'Do not hesitate' opts for the doubt sense. But the discriminating sense is equally present: do not make ethnic/purity distinctions in going with them. The ambiguity is productive: Peter must both overcome his doubt and overcome his ethnic-discrimination reflex. 'I have sent them' — the Spirit claims Cornelius's messengers as his own ambassadors.
21: 'Peter went down and said to the men, I'm the one you're looking for. Why have you come?' — the directness of Peter's question (tis hē aitia di' hēn pārestethe — what is the reason for which you have come?) uses legal/formal vocabulary (aitia = cause, reason; paristēmi = to present oneself officially). Peter is processing the vision while meeting visitors; his directness may reflect his attempt to understand the connection between the vision and these Gentile visitors.
22: 'A righteous and God-fearing man' (dikaios... kai phoboumenos ton theon) — dikaios (disp=4: lit=righteous, tho=just) applied to Cornelius is the highest evaluation available. The MKT 'righteous' preserves the OT covenant-standing sense: Cornelius is dikaios not merely in the moral sense but in the sense of one whose life aligns with God's standard. This is the same word used for John the Baptist's parents (Luke 1:6) and Simeon (Luke 2:25) — the OT-covenant-righteous who are ready to receive messianic fulfillment.
23: 'The next day Peter started out with them, and some of the believers from Joppa went along' — the 'six brothers' of 11:12 accompany Peter, providing the legal-testimony minimum (Deut 19:15 — two or three witnesses). Peter does not go alone; the witness-cluster that accompanies him will be the evidence of what happened at Cornelius's house. Luke's mention of their going along anticipates the role they will play in Peter's Jerusalem defense (11:12 — 'these six brothers also came with me').
24: 'Cornelius was expecting them and had called together his relatives and close friends' — Cornelius's gathering of relatives (syngeneis) and close friends (ananagkaious philous — necessary/close friends; ananankaios can mean 'compelled by close ties') mirrors the communal-reception pattern of Acts conversions: Lydia and her household (16:15), the Philippian jailer and his household (16:34). The gospel regularly arrives at households, not merely to individuals. The household is the social unit of reception.
25: 'As Peter entered the house, Cornelius met him and fell at his feet in reverence' (prosekunēsen — worshipped/did obeisance) — proskuneō is the standard Greek word for obeisance before divine figures, kings, and extraordinary persons. Cornelius does not necessarily intend to worship Peter as a deity; he may be performing the highest available honorific gesture before a divine messenger. Peter's immediate refusal (v.26) is the apostolic pattern: the servant deflects worship from himself to the Lord he represents.
26: 'Peter made him get up. Stand up, I am only a man myself' (anastēthi kai gar autēs anthrōpos eimi — rise, for I myself am also a man) — the kai gar autēs (I myself also) makes Peter's humanness explicit: I too belong to the human category; worshipping me misdirects the reverence that belongs to the Lord. The same deflection appears with Paul and Barnabas at Lystra (14:14-15). The refusal of worship is one of the consistent apostolic behaviors that distinguishes the apostles from the divine figures they serve.
27: 'Talking with him, Peter went inside and found a large gathering of people' — the conversation (synaphistanē autō — talking/conversing with him) as they walked in mirrors the dialogue-as-relationship pattern of the Emmaus road (Luke 24:14-15). Peter enters the Gentile house in conversation — the boundary-crossing is enacted dialogically before the formal teaching begins. The 'large gathering' (sunelēluthotas pollous) demonstrates that Cornelius's faithful obedience has produced a ready harvest.
28: 'You are well aware that it is against our law for a Jew to associate with or visit a Gentile' — Peter's explanation uses the language of his own formation: athemiton (against divine/natural law, unlawful) refers to prohibitions believed to be foundational to the social order. 'But God has shown me that I should not call anyone impure or unclean' — Peter's interpretation of the vision now becomes explicit: the vision was not about food but about people. The transition from food-categories to person-categories is the theological move the vision required.
29: 'So when I was sent for, I came without raising any objection. May I ask why you sent for me?' — the logos Peter asks about (dia tina logon — for what word/reason) uses logos (disp=3) in the 'reason/cause' sense rather than the Johannine 'Word.' The MKT 'why' accurately captures this contextual usage. Peter comes 'without raising any objection' (anadikritōs — without making a distinction/without doubting) — using the same diakrinō root as the Spirit's command in v.20.
30: 'Four days ago I was in my house praying at this hour, at three in the afternoon' — Cornelius's precise temporal reconstruction (apo tetartēs hēmeras mechri tautēs tēs hōras — from the fourth day until this hour) provides verifiable timeline. The 'three in the afternoon' prayer confirms that Cornelius was observing the Jewish temple prayer-hour — the God-fearer's participation in Jewish liturgical time even without temple access. The angel appeared at the very hour of his prayer.
31: 'Cornelius, your prayer has been heard and your gifts to the poor have been remembered before God' — mnēsthēsan (were remembered, aorist passive) uses the divine-memory verb from mnēmosunon (memorial offering) in v.4: God has remembered/called-to-mind Cornelius's prayer and alms. The divine remembering is the OT language of covenant attention: God 'remembered' Noah (Gen 8:1), Abraham (Gen 19:29), Rachel (Gen 30:22), Hannah (1 Sam 1:19). Cornelius's prayer has entered the divine memorial-register.
32: 'Now send to Joppa for Simon who is called Peter. He is a guest in the home of Simon the tanner, who lives by the sea' — the repetition of the full address (same as 10:5-6) serves as Luke's verification that the angelic instruction was specific and accurate. The narrative double-report (angel's instruction → Cornelius's report to Peter) is Luke's authentication technique: the story that can be confirmed from two sides is the reliable story.
33: 'Now we are all here in the presence of God (enōpion tou theou — before God) to listen to everything the Lord has commanded you to tell us' — ta prostetagmena soi hypo tou Kyriou (all the things commanded you by the Lord) — Cornelius presents his assembled household as ready-recipients of a divine command delivered through Peter. 'In the presence of God' positions the entire gathering as a theophanic assembly: they are not merely listening to a speaker but standing before God while a messenger delivers divine instruction.
35: 'Everyone who fears him and does what is right (dikaiosynēn ergazomenos — working righteousness)' — dikaiosynē (disp=4: lit=righteousness, tho=justification) here means practical covenant-alignment: the one who produces righteousness as a way of life. The MKT renders this 'does what is right' — the active, behavioral sense. This is the dikaiosynē that Cornelius embodies: not the forensic justification of Romans but the practical moral alignment with God's standards that genuine fear of God produces. 'Is acceptable to him' (dektos autō estin) — the same word used in Luke 4:24 for the prophet's acceptance in his homeland.
36: 'The message God sent to the people of Israel, announcing the good news of peace through Jesus Christ' — the logos (disp=3) in ton logon hon apesteilen is the 'message/word' in the proclamation sense: the specific content of the gospel announcement. The MKT 'message' captures the noun's proclamation-content sense. 'Peace through Jesus Christ' (eirēnēn dia Iēsou Christou) — the preached peace echoes Isa 52:7 ('how beautiful are the feet of him who brings good news, who publishes peace'). 'He is Lord of all' (houtos estin pantōn Kyrios) — the 'all' (pantōn) includes Gentiles: the Lord's domain is universal, not ethnically restricted.
37: 'You know what has happened throughout the province of Judaea, beginning in Galilee after the baptism that John preached' — the Cornelius-audience is assumed to have some knowledge of the Jesus-movement (the widespread character of Jesus's ministry made it regionally known). Peter's sermon begins from what they already know and builds toward what they do not know (the resurrection). The 'beginning in Galilee' framework matches the Gospel's narrative arc (Luke 4:14 — the Galilean beginning of Jesus's public ministry).
38: 'How God anointed (echrisen — anointed) Jesus of Nazareth with the Holy Spirit and power' — chriō (disp=2 for pneuma=Spirit) is the anointing-verb from which Christos (anointed/Christ) derives. The MKT 'anointed' correctly preserves the verbal root that names Jesus as the Christ: the naming and the action are etymologically continuous. The anointing is specified as the Jordan-baptism anointing (the Spirit descending, Luke 3:22). 'Who went around doing good (euergetōn — being a benefactor) and healing all who were under the power of the devil' — euergetōn is the benefactor-language of Hellenistic civic culture: Jesus is framed as the ultimate civic benefactor who heals rather than merely honors.
39: 'We are witnesses of everything he did in the country of the Jews and in Jerusalem' — the 'we' of apostolic testimony (hēmeis martyres — we witnesses) establishes the apostolic witness-claim on which the sermon's credibility rests. 'They killed him by hanging him on a cross (kremesantes epi xylou — hanging on a tree)' — xylon (tree/wood) rather than stauros (cross) is the word chosen for its LXX resonance: Deut 21:23 ('anyone who is hung on a tree is under God's curse'). Paul explicitly applies this to the crucifixion in Gal 3:13. The tree-hanging language frames the cross in its covenantal-curse context.
40: 'But God raised him from the dead on the third day and caused him to be seen' — the passive ēgeiren (raised; God did the raising) and emphanē genesthai (to become visible/manifest) frame the resurrection as a divine act of presentation: God both raised him and made him visible. The resurrection is not merely an event that happened; it is an event that God caused to be witnessed. The 'third day' formula (tē tritē hēmera) is the Passion prediction's fulfillment-marker (Luke 9:22; 18:33; 24:7, 46).
41: 'Not to all the people, but to witnesses whom God had already chosen' — the selectivity of the resurrection appearances (ou panti tō laō — not to all the people) is stated explicitly here. God chose specific witnesses (prokeheirotonēmenois — previously appointed/elected, a rare term in the NT) to whom the risen Christ appeared. 'We who ate and drank with him after he rose from the dead' — the bodily resurrection is authenticated by table-fellowship: eating and drinking with the risen Christ (Luke 24:41-43 — he ate a piece of broiled fish; Acts 1:4 — eating with them).
42: 'He commanded us to preach to the people and to testify that he is the one whom God appointed as judge of the living and the dead (kritēs zōntōn kai nekrōn — judge of the living and the dead)' — the appointed-judge formula echoes Dan 7:13-14 (the Son of Man given authority to judge all peoples). 'Living and dead' extends the judgment's scope beyond the present generation. The resurrection (v.40) is what qualifies Jesus for the judge-role: the one who conquered death is qualified to preside over the judgment of both the living and the dead.
43: 'All the prophets testify about him that everyone who believes in him receives forgiveness of sins through his name' — the apostolic appeal to prophetic testimony (pantes hoi prophētai — all the prophets) is a sweeping claim: the entire prophetic corpus points to Christ. 'Everyone who believes' (panta ton pisteuonta — everyone who believes) is the universal-scope formulation that prepares for the Spirit's fall on the uncircumcised Gentiles in the next verse. The faith-criterion displaces the ethnicity-criterion as the condition for receiving forgiveness.
45: 'The circumcised believers who had come with Peter were astonished that the gift of the Holy Spirit had been poured out even on Gentiles' — epechythē (had been poured out — aorist passive of ekcheō, the same verb as Joel 2:28 LXX 'I will pour out my Spirit') connects the Cornelius Pentecost explicitly to the Joel-Pentecost promise. 'Circumcised believers' (hoi ek peritomēs pistoi — the faithful ones of the circumcision) marks the Jewish-Christian observers as the legitimate witnesses to what happened: their astonishment is the evidence's authentication.
46: 'For they heard them speaking in tongues and praising God' — the two signs (glossais lalountōn — speaking with tongues; megalunontōn ton theon — magnifying God) are the same twin-evidence as Pentecost (2:4 — spoke in other tongues; 2:11 — 'we hear them telling in our own tongues the mighty works of God'). The tongue-speech is the Spirit's authentication in recognizable form to the Jewish observers. 'Then Peter said' — Peter's question follows immediately from the evidence; he does not deliberate or delay.
47: 'Surely no one can stand in the way of their being baptised with water?' (mēti to hydōr kōlusai dynatai tis — can anyone withhold/prevent the water?) — kōlyō (to prevent/withhold) is the key baptism-verb in Acts: Philip uses it in the Ethiopian eunuch's baptism (8:36 — 'what prevents me from being baptized?'). The inability to prevent water-baptism follows from the inability to prevent Spirit-baptism that has already occurred. The Spirit's gift precedes the water-seal; the water-seal cannot be withheld after the Spirit has already given the inward reality.
48: 'So he ordered that they be baptised in the name of Jesus Christ' — the command-form (prosetaxen) makes baptism an apostolic command, not merely an option. 'In the name of Jesus Christ' (en tō onomati Iēsou Christou) — the single-name formula (vs. the threefold formula of Matt 28:19) is the standard Acts baptism-formula; both formulas identify the same trinitarian authority. 'Then they asked Peter to stay with them for a few days' — the post-baptismal table-fellowship that follows (implied in the staying) is the seal of the social integration that the Spirit-gift and baptism have enacted.
